coredata.dc:title: A nanocomposite optosensing probe based on hierarchical porous carbon and graphene quantum dots incorporated in selective polymer for the detection of trace ofloxacin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Suanchan, Kanokrat
Chansud, Nutnicha
Sa-nguanprang, Surisa
Bunkoed, Opas
coredata.dc:description: A hierarchical nanocomposite optosensing probe was designed and fabricated to detect ofloxacin. The nanocomposite probe was fabricated by incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and hierarchical porous carbon (HPC) into molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). The synthesized HPC and GQDs, and the composite probe were characterized. The fabrication procedure of the probe and detection conditions were investigated to obtain the best sensitivity and fast detection. In the optimized condition, the developed nanocomposite probe was ultrasensitive and selective toward ofloxacin while remaining unaffected by structural analogue compounds. The probe showed good linear range for ofloxacin from 0.10 to 25 μg L−1 with a limit of detection of 0.066 μg L−1. Applied to detect ofloxacin in milks, the probe was accurate, achieving recoveries in the range of 92–99% with an RSD <7%. The analysis results of spiked milk samples agreed well with HPLC analysis. The optosensing system is cost-effective, simple and could be modified and applied as an alternative method to detect trace compounds in various samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Probe
Fluorescence quenching
Porous carbon
Composite

Effect of pH of dispersion solution
Effect of mole ratio of TM to MN to CL
Analysis of milk sample and comparison with HPLC technique

The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis procedure of the composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe for ofloxacin detection.

The SEM image (A) is of HPC: the inset is a photograph of HPC. Adsorption-desorption isotherms are of HPC (B). The SEM image (C) and size distribution histogram of the nanocomposite HPC-GQDs-MIP probe (D). A photograph of the probe in the presence (left) and absence (right) of ofloxacin (E). The FTIR spectra (F) are of HPC (a) and the nanocomposite HPC-GQDs-MIP probe (b).

Effect of incubation time on the fluorescence emission of the probe (A). The effects on probe sensitivity of dispersion solution pH (B), HPC loading (C) and the mole ratio of template to monomer to cross-linker (D).
The sensitivity of various composite nanoprobes for the detection of ofloxacin.
Fluorescence spectra of the composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe (A) and a composite HPC-GQDs-NIP nanoprobe for the detection of ofloxacin (B).

HPLC chromatograms are of ofloxacin at different concentrations in milk samples (A): (a) 0.0, (b) 2.0, (c) 5.0, (d) 10.0 and (e) 20.0 μg L−1. The plot (B) shows the correlation of results obtained with the optosensing system and with the HPLC method, the data include four milk sample with three replicates for each concentrations.
The determination of ofloxacin in milks using the nano-probe based on the composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe (n = 3).
Comparison of the analytical performance of the nano-probe based on composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe and other methods for the determination of ofloxacin.

row.entry: Fluorometry based on AuNPs quenching RB

head.ce:title: A nanocomposite optosensing probe based on hierarchical porous carbon and graphene quantum dots incorporated in selective polymer for the detection of trace ofloxacin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry, Division of Physical Science, Faculty of Science, Prince of Songkla University, Hat Yai, Songkhla 90110, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A hierarchical nanocomposite optosensing probe was designed and fabricated to detect ofloxacin. The nanocomposite probe was fabricated by incorporating graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and hierarchical porous carbon (HPC) into molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). The synthesized HPC and GQDs, and the composite probe were characterized. The fabrication procedure of the probe and detection conditions were investigated to obtain the best sensitivity and fast detection. In the optimized condition, the developed nanocomposite probe was ultrasensitive and selective toward ofloxacin while remaining unaffected by structural analogue compounds. The probe showed good linear range for ofloxacin from 0.10 to 25 μg L−1 with a limit of detection of 0.066 μg L−1. Applied to detect ofloxacin in milks, the probe was accurate, achieving recoveries in the range of 92–99% with an RSD <7%. The analysis results of spiked milk samples agreed well with HPLC analysis. The optosensing system is cost-effective, simple and could be modified and applied as an alternative method to detect trace compounds in various samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Ofloxacin is a fluoroquinolones antibiotic widely used in the treatment of infectious diseases of humans and animals [1]. However, the overuse of ofloxacin can lead to human health problems such as allergies or drug resistance [2]. Due to its slow rate of biodegradation, ofloxacin can contaminate the environment and leave residues in animal tissues which can reach humans through the food chain [3]. The maximum residue limits (MRLs) for fluoroquinolones in food have been at 100 µg kg−1. Therefore, the monitoring of ofloxacin in foods such as meat and milk is important for food safety.

Several analytical methods have been reported to detect ofloxacin, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4,5], fluorescence [6,7], aptasensors [8,9], luminescence [10], colorimetric [11] and electrochemical detection [12,13]. Among these methods, fluorescence spectroscopy-based probes have attracted considerable interest due to their cost-effective equipment, simple operation and fast response [14]. To detect ofloxacin, the application of a fluorescent probe is possible but, since ofloxacin is present in food at low concentrations and the matrix is often highly complex, the sensitivity and selectivity of detection would require enhancement. This type of enhancement has been achieved by integrating good optical materials with highly specific materials to produce a composite sensing probe that improved both the sensitivity and selectivity of detection [15]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are an interesting optical material of low toxicity. The high fluorescence intensity, good chemical stability and large surface to mass ratio of GQDs improved the sensitivity of a sensing probe [16] and GQDs have been applied as a sensing material to detect target compounds that include tetracycline [17], dopamine [18], cholesterol [19] and mercury ions [20]. The selectivity of a composite sensing probe can also be enhanced using molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP), which is synthesized in the presence of the target analyte as a template. The removal of the template creates cavities which are specific to the target analyte by functional groups, size and shape [21]. MIPs have been applied as highly selective materials in applications such as an adsorbent [22], electrode modification for electrochemical sensor [23] and a fluorescent nanosensor [24]. The adsorption ability of composite sensing probes can also be improved by the incorporation of a high affinity material. For the adsorption of ofloxacin, the incorporation of hierarchical porous carbon (HPC) is a good choice of affinity material since it can adsorb ofloxacin via π-π interaction and hydrogen bonding. HPC is a porous material that provides a large surface area for adsorption of target compounds [25].

In this work, an ultrasensitive and selective probe was designed for ofloxacin detection. The nanocomposite sensing probe integrated the high adsorption ability of HPC, the good fluorescence properties of GQDs and the good selectivity of MIP (HPC-GQDs-MIP). The fabrication of the nanocomposite probe and the detection system were investigated to achieve the highest sensitivity within a short analysis time. The developed probe was characterized and the quenching mechanism was evaluated. The probe was used to detect ofloxacin in milks. The accuracy was investigated by analysis of spiked real samples and analysis results were compared to analysis by HPLC.
The details of chemical and materials can be found in the Supplementary Information (Sup. Inform).
The details of instruments are provided in the Sup. Inform.

The GQDs were synthesized in a pyrolysis process described in a previous work [26], in which 2.00 g of citric acid were placed in a round bottom flask and heated at 100 °C until the citric acid was melted and a yellow-orange solution was obtained. The solution was then added dropwise into a beaker containing 100 mL of NaOH solution (0.25 M) and stirred at 750 rpm for 30 min. The obtained mixture solution was placed in dialysis bag for 24 h to remove unreacted components. After dialysis, the GQDs were stored at 4 °C until used.

The synthesis of HPC was modified from a previous work [27]. Briefly, rice husk (RH) was cleaned with DI water before dried at 105 °C for 12 h. The dried RH underwent pyrolysis at 500 °C for 1 h under N2 and was then rinsed with 80 mL of 2.5 M NaOH, washed with DI water until runoff pH was neutral and dried at 100 °C for 12 h (RHN). Then, RHN was soaked in 100 mL of 1.0 M KOH and dried at 100 °C for 24 h. The dried RHN then underwent pyrolysis at 800 °C for 30 min under N2 at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. Finally, the synthesized porous HPC was washed with 100 mL of 1.0 M HCl and with DI water and dried at 100 °C for 24 h.

To synthesize the composite sensing probe ( Fig. 1), 20 mL of GQDs and 1.50 mL APTES were mixed in a bottle and continuously stirred for 30 min at 55 °C. Meanwhile, 10 mg of ofloxacin and 0.75 mg of HPC were mixed in 5 mL ethanol and the mixture was poured into the bottle. The mixture solution was continuously stirred for 30 min. Then, 318 μL of TEOS and 200 μL NH4OH were added and the whole was stirred for 4 h. For the removal of template (ofloxacin), the fabricated composite probe was washed three times with 20 mL of ethanol under stirring for 15 min, followed by centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 10 min, after which no template (ofloxacin) presented in the washing solution. The synthesized composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe was dried at 60 °C for 6 h and kept in desiccator until used.

The composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe was dispersed in 200 μL of phosphate buffer at pH 7.0 and mixed with 100 μL of standard solution or sample. The mixture was incubated under rotation for 15 min to enable binding between ofloxacin and nanoprobe. After incubation, the emission intensity was measured between 400 and 700 nm from an excitation of 355 nm. Fluorescence intensity was measured by setting the slit widths at 10 nm for the excitation and emission.

Before analysis with the probe, milks were retreated as follows: 10 mL of milk were centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 15 min to remove fat. Then, 10 mL of acetonitrile were added to precipitate proteins and the sample was again centrifuged for 15 min. The supernatant was separated and evaporated at 60 °C, and the residue was dissolved in 2.0 mL of DI water. The proposed probe was then used to detect ofloxacin in the sample solution. The accuracy of the developed method was performed in the spiked samples. The milk samples were spiked with ofloxacin to final concentrations of 2.0, 5.0, 10.0 and 20 μg L−1.

Spiked milk samples (2.0, 5.0, 10.0 and 20 μg L−1) were also analyzed for ofloxacin by HPLC using the 1100 series system (Agilent Technologies, Germany). The separation column was a Fortis C18 (4.6 mm × 15 cm, 5 µm). The mobile phase was 0.10 M phosphoric acid and acetonitrile at 75:25 (v/v) with a flow rate of 1.0 mL min−1 and an injection volume of 20 μL. The detector was a diode array detector at 254 nm. Standard ofloxacin solutions were spiked in milk samples to final concentrations of 2.0, 5.0, 10.0 and 20 μg L−1 and then extracted as described in Section 2.7. The matrix matched calibration curve was used for quantitative analysis of ofloxacin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of HPC and HPC-QDs-MIP probe
ce:section.ce:para: The UV-Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the nanocomposite HPC-GQDs-MIP probe are shown in Fig. S1 . The emission spectrum of HPC-GQDs-MIP was narrow, symmetric and exhibited a high fluorescence intensity, which is a good property for sensing applications. The maximum emission intensity was observed at 455 nm. The quantum yields (QY) of the synthesized GQDs and the HPC-GQDs-MIP probe were determined using a Rhodamine 6 G standard [28]. The QYs were 0.95 for Rhodamine 6 G, 0.57 for the GQDs and 0.50 for the HPC-GQDs-MIP probe.

An SEM image of HPC ( Fig. 2A) showed the high porosity and large surface area, which was determined to be 869.9015 m2 g−1. The adsorption-desorption isotherm of HPC is shown in Fig. 2B. The SEM image of the HPC-GQDs-MIP probe (Fig. 2C) showed a rough surface by the reason of the presence of recognition sites and diameter of 440–530 nm (Fig. 2D). The photograph (Fig. 2E) shows the composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe with and without of the template under UV light. The blue color visible without ofloxacin was clearly reduced in the presence of ofloxacin. FT-IR was utilized to evaluate the functional groups of HPC and the HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe (Fig. 2F). In the FT-IR spectrum of HPC, the peaks at 1579, 2932 and 3422 cm−1 due to CO, CH and OH stretching, respectively. FT-IR spectrum of the HPC-GQDs-MIP probe presented the absorption peaks of HPC and a broad band at 1058 cm−1 that was according to Si–O–Si stretching of the MIP layer. FT-IR analysis confirmed that the nanocomposite was successfully fabricated.

The nanocomposite HPC-GQDs-MIP probe was prepared through a copolymerization process using ofloxacin as the template (TM), APTES as a monomer (MN) and TEOS as a cross-linker (CL) in the presence of HPC. Ofloxacin, APTES, GQDs and HPC self-assembled through hydrogen bonding and π-π interaction. HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoparticles were produced when the CL and a catalyst (NH4OH) were added to the mixture solution. Before the removal of ofloxacin, the emission intensity of the HPC-GQDs-MIP probe was about 25% that of the nanocomposite HPC-GQDs-NIP probe. After template removal, the intensity was restored to almost the same level of the nanocomposite HPC-GQDs-NIP probe (Fig. S2) which can confirm that the template was completely removed from the HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe and the composite probe could successfully bind with ofloxacin.

To achieve the highest sensitivity for the detection of ofloxacin with the HPC-GQDs-MIP probe, affecting parameters were optimized, including the incubation time, pH of the dispersion solution, amount of HPC and the ratio of TM to MN to CL.

To achieve complete binding between ofloxacin and the recognition cavities, the adsorption time between ofloxacin and the HPC-GQDs-MIP probe was investigated from 2 min to 30 min. The emission intensity of the nanocomposite probe changed with increasing incubation time from 2 to 15 min and then constant with further increases in incubation time ( Fig. 3A). Thus, 15 min was selected to detect ofloxacin using the HPC-GQDs-MIP probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH of dispersion solution
ce:section.ce:para: The HPC-GQDs-MIP probe was dispersed in phosphate buffer solution before being mixed with the sample solution. Since the pH of the buffer might influence the sensitivity of the probe toward ofloxacin, the sensitivity of the probe was investigated at pH 5.0, 6.0, 7.0 and 8.0. The sensitivity of the probe was highest at pH 7.0 (Fig. 3B). Sensitivity was lower at pH below 7.0 due to the degradation of ofloxacin in the acidic solution [29]. Sensitivity was also low at pH above 7.0, due to the instability of the silica layer of MIP, which destroyed the recognition sites of the MIP probe. Therefore, the nanocomposite HPC-GQDs-MIP probe was dispersed in buffer solution at pH 7.0 for the detection of ofloxacin.

The effect on sensitivity of the amount of HPC used to prepare the HPC-GQDs-MIP probe was evaluated from 0.25 to 1.00 mg. The sensitivity of ofloxacin detection increased as the amount of HPC was increased from 0.25 to 0.75 mg and decreased at higher loadings of HPC (Fig. 3C). Low sensitivity was achieved at low loadings of HPC due to the low number of adsorption sites on the probe. Too high a loading of HPC led to the stacking of HPC through the π-π interaction, which reduced the specific surface area of the probe. Therefore, 0.75 mg of HPC was used to fabricate the HPC-GQDs-MIP probe for ofloxacin detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of mole ratio of TM to MN to CL
ce:section.ce:para: The components for the synthesis of MIP included the TM, a MN and a CL. Each component can influence the formation of recognition sites on the composite probe and affect sensitivity toward ofloxacin. Thus, the mole ratio of TM: MN: CL was optimized. The results showed that the sensitivity of the probe was highest at a TM: MN: CL mole ratio of 1: 6: 55 (Fig. 3D). Sensitivity was lower at lower amounts of MN by the reason of low number of -NH2 groups to interact with the target. Meanwhile, sensitivity was also low at larger amounts of MN because excess MN disturbed the formation of recognition sites. The amount of CL also effected the sensitivity of ofloxacin. Lower amounts of CL produced low sensitivity due to the formation of a weak MIP layer and excess CL also disrupted the formation of recognition sites. Thus, the optimal mole ratio of TM: MN: CL was 1: 6: 55 for the fabrication of the HPC-GQDs-MIP probe.

The sensitivity of various nanocomposite probes was evaluated for the detection of ofloxacin. The probes included the HPC-GQDs-MIP, a GQDs-MIP, an HPC-GQDs-NIP and a GQDs-NIP nanoprobe. The HPC-GQDs-MIP exhibited the highest sensitivity for the detection of ofloxacin ( Fig. 4) due to the specific binding sites of MIP and affinity binding could occur between HPC and ofloxacin. The BET surface areas of HPC-GQDs-MIP and GQDs-MIP nanoprobes were 16.5163 m2 g−1 and 12.6083 m2 g−1, respectively. These results confirm that HPC can improve surface areas of nanocomposite probe for the adsorption of ofloxacin. HPC can adsorb ofloxacin via hydrogen bonding and π-π interaction. The GQDs-NIP nanoprobe exhibited the lowest sensitivity due to no specific recognition sites for the binding with ofloxacin.

Before applying the developed optosensing system to real sample analysis, the analytical performances were investigated in terms of linearity, limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantification (LOQ) and repeatability. The emission intensity of the composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe decreased linearly with the increasing ofloxacin from 0.10 to 25.0 μg L−1 with a R2 of 0.9938. Fig. 5A and B show the respective fluorescence emission spectra of the composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe and the composite HPC-GQDs-NIP nanoprobe. The LOD (3SD/slope) and LOQ (10 SD/slope) of the developed probe were 0.066 and 0.23 μg L−1, respectively. The method repeatability was investigated in term of intra-day and inter-day precision with was performed within a day (n = 6) and among days (n = 6), respectively. The RSDs of sensitivity were 1.9% for intra-day and 2.4% for inter-day precision. These results confirmed that the probe could be applied for ultrasensitive trace detection of ofloxacin with good repeatability.

The specificity of the HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe was determined as the imprinting factor (IF), which was obtained from Ksv(MIP)/Ksv(NIP). The IF of ofloxacin was 6.35. The linear coefficient of determination of nanoprobe to ofloxacin determination is 0.9938, indicating that the result was in good agreement with Stern-Volmer equation [30]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of the HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe can be described by the Stern-Volmer equation [31], F0/F = 1 + Ksv [C], where F0 and F are the emission intensity in the absence and presence of ofloxacin, respectively, Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant and [C] is the concentration of ofloxacin.

The quenching mechanism can be elucidated by molecular orbital theory. The electrons of GQDs can be excited from ground state to excited state, without template ofloxacin around the HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe, the excited electron of GQDs return to the valence band with producing fluorescence. While, in the presence of template molecule (ofloxacin), the NH2 groups of the functional monomer (APTES) can interact with carbonyl of ofloxacin to form a Meisenheimer complex through hydrogen bonding. The interaction would lead to electron transfer between GQDs and ofloxacin leading to fluorescence quenching. This conclusion was supported by the separation of the absorption spectrum of ofloxacin from the emission spectrum of the composite HPC-GQDs-MIP probe (Fig. S3). The two spectra did not overlap, indicating that the mechanism was not because of energy transfer [32]. Ofloxacin shows an apparent absorption band overlap with the excitation spectrum of the HPC-GQDs-MIP sensing probe as demonstrated in Fig. S4. So, the quenching mechanism was an electron transfer process that occurred when ofloxacin adsorbed and bound with recognition sites of the probe.

The selectivity of the nano-probe based on composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe was evaluated before analyzing real samples. The probe was used to detect ofloxacin and analogue structures (lomefloxacin, ciprofloxacin, marbofloxacin and sarafloxacin). As demonstrated in Fig. S5A, the composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe showed high sensitivity toward ofloxacin but low sensitivity toward the analogue compounds. Sensitivity was the slope of calibration curve by plotting between F0/F and concentration of ofloxacin and analogue structures at 2.0, 5.0, 10.0 and 20 μg L−1. The results implied that the composite probe was selective toward ofloxacin. In contrast, the composite NIP probe showed low sensitivity to both ofloxacin and the analogue structures because of the absence of recognition sites. Only non-specific binding could occur. To confirm the specificity of the developed composite probe, a competitive study was carried out. The composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe was used to detect ofloxacin in the presence of different levels of sarafloxacin (Fig. S5B). The results indicated that increasing the concentration of sarafloxacin did not change sensitivity. The results confirmed the selectivity and specificity of the developed composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe.

To investigate the stability of the developed optosensing system, the HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe was dispersed in buffer at pH 7.0 and the fluorescence emission of the probe was monitored over time. The emission intensity of the probe did not change until 300 min had elapsed. The response was still higher than 95% (Fig. S6A). The result implied that the stability of the nanoprobe was good under detection conditions. The fabrication reproducibility of the sensing probe was also investigated by fabrication six different probes in the same condition and detecting ofloxacin with each preparation. The RSD of average sensitivity was 2.5% (n = 6) (Fig. S6B).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of milk sample and comparison with HPLC technique
ce:section.ce:para: The composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe was applied to determine ofloxacin in milk. Ofloxacin was detected in some samples. The concentration range detected was 0.50–0.62 μg L−1. The accuracy of the nano-probe was also investigated by analysis of milk spiked with ofloxacin at different concentrations and relative recovery was considered, which was calculated from the following equation: Relative recovery (%) = (CF-CS/CA) × 100, where CF is the total ofloxacin found in a spiked milk, CS is the ofloxacin in the original milk sample and CA is the standard ofloxacin spiked in milk. As summarized in Table 1, good recoveries were achieved in the range of 92.4–99.9%. The relative standard deviation of indeterminate error lower than 7%.

The analysis of milk using the proposed probe was compared with analysis using HPLC. Chromatograms of spiked milk at different levels are shown in Fig. 6A. The LOD and LOQ of ofloxacin in milk sample based on signal-to-noise ratio of 3 and 10 were 0.7 and 2.0 μg L−1, respectively. The results obtained from the probe agreed well with the HPLC with a determination coefficient of 0.9908 (Fig. 6B). Therefore, the probe based on the composite HPC-GQDs-MIP nanoprobe can be utilized as a sensitive and selective method to detect ofloxacin with good accuracy and precision.

By comparison with previous works, the probe provided a lower LOD ( Table 2). The recovery and precision were better or comparable with other methods. Other advantages of the probe over other methods were the simple and rapid detection procedure and the cost-effective instrumentation. The proposed probe uses a small volume of sample and aqueous buffer solution in the detection process. While, the liquid chromatographic method requires large volume of organic solvent as a mobile phase.

A composite of hierarchical porous carbon, GQDs and MIP was fabricated for the detection of ofloxacin in an optosensing system. Integrating of hierarchical porous carbon, the good fluorescence properties of GQDs and the good selectivity of MIP produced an ultrasensitive and selective nano-probe with a rapid detection response. The developed method was utilized to detect ofloxacin in milks. Good accuracy and precision were achieved. The results obtained with the nano-probe using the nanocomposite probe agreed well with the results obtained from HPLC. This probe can be utilized as an alternative, highly sensitive and selective strategy for the detection of ofloxacin in food samples which contain strong matrix interferences. The fabrication procedure of the nanocomposite sensing probe can also be modified and applied to detect other trace organic compounds.

Kanokrat Suanchan: Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft, Visualization. Nutnicha Chansud: Methodology, Writing. Surisa Sa-nguanprang: Methodology, Writing. Opas Bunkoed: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data Curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Council of Thailand (NRCT5-RSA63022-03), the Center of Excellence for Innovation in Chemistry (PERCH-CIC), Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation. The authors thank Mr. Thomas Duncan Coyne for English proofreading.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2021.127376.

Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective extraction and enhanced-sensitivity detection of fluoroquinolones in swine body fluids by liquid chromatography–high resolution mass spectrometry: application in long-term monitoring in livestock
Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Wang, S. Li, D. Chen, Y. Zhao, Y. Wu, K. Qi, Selective extraction and enhanced-sensitivity detection of fluoroquinolones in swine body fluids by liquid chromatography–high resolution mass spectrometry: Application in long-term monitoring in livestock, Food Chem. 341 (2021) 128269. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.128269.

Antibiotics

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Occurrence and toxicity of antibiotics in the aquatic environment: a review
Chemosphere

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Use of a magnetic covalent organic framework material with a large specific surface area as an effective adsorbent for the extraction and determination of six fluoroquinolone antibiotics by HPLC in milk sample
J. Sep. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Guan, H. Wu, L. Yang, Z. Wang, J. Wu, Use of a magnetic covalent organic framework material with a large specific surface area as an effective adsorbent for the extraction and determination of six fluoroquinolone antibiotics by HPLC in milk sample, J. Sep. Sci. 43(19) (2020) 3775‐3784. https://doi.org/10.1002/jssc.202000616.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction using a switchable polarity dispersive solvent. Automated HPLC-FLD determination of ofloxacin in chicken meat
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Timofeeva, S. Timofeev, L. Moskvin, A. Bulatov, A dispersive liquid-liquid microextraction using a switchable polarity dispersive solvent. Automated HPLC-FLD determination of ofloxacin in chicken meat, Anal. Chim. Acta 949 (2017) 35‐42. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.11.018.
